stem: Find the absolute maximum value of \( f(x, y) = [[a]](x^{2} + y^{2}) + ([[c]]) \) over the closed disk \( x^{2} + y^{2} \le [[r]]^{2} \).
solution: Since \( [[a]] > 0 \), \( f \) increases with \( x^{2}+y^{2} \), so its maximum on the disk occurs on the boundary where \( x^{2}+y^{2} = [[r]]^{2} \). There \( f = [[a]]([[r]]^{2}) + ([[c]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the minimum value (attained at the center), not the maximum.
This uses the radius \( [[r]] \) instead of \( [[r]]^{2} \) for \( x^{2}+y^{2} \) on the boundary.
This forgets to add the constant \( ([[c]]) \).
This subtracts the constant instead of adding it.
This doubles the boundary contribution.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x, y) = [[a]]x + [[b]]y + ([[c]]) \) on the closed rectangle \( [[x0]] \le x \le [[x1]],\ [[y0]] \le y \le [[y1]] \). Since \( f \) is linear, it has no interior critical points, so its absolute maximum occurs at a corner of the rectangle. What is that absolute maximum value?
solution: Because \( [[a]] > 0 \) and \( [[b]] > 0 \), \( f \) increases as \( x \) and \( y \) increase, so the maximum over the rectangle occurs at the corner with the largest \( x \) and \( y \), namely \( ([[x1]], [[y1]]) \). There \( f = [[a]]([[x1]]) + [[b]]([[y1]]) + ([[c]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the minimum value, attained at the opposite corner \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \).
This uses the corner \( ([[x0]], [[y1]]) \), which mixes the smallest \( x \) with the largest \( y \).
This uses the corner \( ([[x1]], [[y0]]) \), which mixes the largest \( x \) with the smallest \( y \).
This uses the side lengths of the rectangle instead of the actual corner coordinates.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x, y) = -[[a]](x - ([[p]]))^{2} - [[b]](y - ([[q]]))^{2} + ([[k]]) \) on the closed rectangle \( [[x0]] \le x \le [[x1]],\ [[y0]] \le y \le [[y1]] \). The point \( ([[p]], [[q]]) \) lies in the interior of this rectangle. What is the absolute maximum value of \( f \) on the rectangle?
solution: Since the coefficients of the squared terms are negative, \( f \) is a downward-opening dome with its unique interior critical point at \( ([[p]], [[q]]) \), which is a local (and here global) maximum. Because \( ([[p]], [[q]]) \) lies inside the rectangle, this interior value beats every boundary value, so the absolute maximum is \( f([[p]], [[q]]) = [[k]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the value at the corner \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \), which is smaller than the interior maximum.
This is the value at the corner \( ([[x1]], [[y1]]) \), which is smaller than the interior maximum.
This negates the correct constant term.
This flips the sign of the squared terms, as if the paraboloid opened upward.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: A furniture maker splits a work week into \( x \) hours building chairs and \( y \) hours building tables, subject to \( x \ge 0 \), \( y \ge 0 \), and \( x + y \le [[k]] \) (total hours available). The week's profit, in dollars, is \( P(x, y) = [[p]]x + [[q]]y + ([[c]]) \). Over this triangular region of allowed \( (x, y) \) pairs, what is the maximum possible profit?
solution: Since \( P \) is linear, its maximum over the triangle with vertices \( (0,0) \), \( ([[k]], 0) \), \( (0, [[k]]) \) occurs at a vertex. Because \( [[p]] > [[q]] \), spending all hours on chairs beats spending all hours on tables, so the maximum is at \( (x,y)=([[k]],0) \): \( P = [[p]]([[k]]) + ([[c]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the vertex \( (0,[[k]]) \), putting all hours into tables instead of the more profitable chairs.
This is the value at the origin vertex \( (0,0) \), where no hours are worked at all.
This wrongly adds the contributions of both pure-chair and pure-table vertices together.
This averages the two coefficients instead of picking the larger one at the correct vertex.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x, y) = -[[a]](x - ([[px]]))^{2} - [[b]](y - ([[py]]))^{2} + ([[k]]) \) on the closed rectangle \( [[x0]] \le x \le [[x1]],\ [[y0]] \le y \le [[y1]] \). What is the absolute maximum value of \( f \) on this rectangle?
solution: The unconstrained critical point \( ([[px]], [[py]]) \) lies OUTSIDE the rectangle (its \( x \)-coordinate \( [[px]] \) is less than \( [[x0]] \)), so the maximum is not attained in the interior. Since \( a>0 \), \( f \) decreases as \( x \) moves away from \( [[px]] \); the closest allowed \( x \) is \( x=[[x0]] \). Since \( [[py]] \) IS interior to \( [[y0]] \le y \le [[y1]] \), the best \( y \) is \( y=[[py]] \). So the maximum is on the left edge at \( ([[x0]], [[py]]) \): \( f = -[[a]]([[x0]]-([[px]]))^{2} + [[k]] = -[[a]]([[gap]])^{2} + [[k]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes the unconstrained critical point \( ([[px]],[[py]]) \) lies inside the rectangle, which it does not.
This uses the far edge \( x=[[x1]] \) instead of the near edge \( x=[[x0]] \) that is actually closest to \( [[px]] \).
This wrongly also penalizes the \( y \)-direction, as if \( y=[[py]] \) were not achievable in the interior.
This wrongly penalizes the \( y \)-direction using the other half-width \( [[wy2]] \).
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x, y) = [[c]]x + [[b]]y \) on the closed region bounded below by \( y = 0 \) and above by the parabola \( y = [[k]] - x^{2} \), for \( -[[m]] \le x \le [[m]] \). What is the absolute maximum value of \( f \) on this region?
solution: For fixed \( x \), since \( [[b]]>0 \), \( f \) increases with \( y \), so the maximum in \( y \) is on the top boundary \( y=[[k]]-x^{2} \). Substituting gives \( g(x) = [[c]]x + [[b]]([[k]]-x^{2}) \), a downward parabola in \( x \) maximized where \( g'(x)=[[c]]-2([[b]])x=0 \), i.e. \( x = [[xstar]] \), which lies in \( (-[[m]], [[m]]) \). The maximum value is \( g([[xstar]]) = [[b]]([[xstar]]^{2}+[[k]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This evaluates \( g \) at the right endpoint \( x=[[m]] \) instead of at the true interior maximizer \( x=[[xstar]] \).
This evaluates \( g \) at the left endpoint \( x=-[[m]] \) instead of at the true interior maximizer.
This assumes the maximum occurs at \( x=0 \), dropping the linear term entirely.
This uses \( y=[[k]] \) (the boundary's value at \( x=0 \)) instead of the correct \( y=[[k]]-x^{2} \) at \( x=[[xstar]] \).
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x, y) = [[a]](x^{2} + y^{2}) + ([[c]]) \) on the closed annular (washer-shaped) region \( [[r1]] \le \sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}} \le [[r2]] \), between two concentric circles. What is the absolute MINIMUM value of \( f \) on this region?
solution: Since \( [[a]] > 0 \), \( f \) increases with distance from the origin, so it is minimized where \( \sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}} \) is as SMALL as possible. The origin itself is excluded (the inner circle has radius \( [[r1]] > 0 \)), so the smallest achievable distance is the inner radius \( [[r1]] \). There \( f = [[a]]([[r1]])^{2} + ([[c]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the outer radius \( [[r2]] \), which gives the MAXIMUM of \( f \) on the annulus, not the minimum.
This assumes the origin (where \( f \) would be smallest without the hole) lies in the region, but it is excluded.
This uses the average of the two radii instead of the smaller (inner) one.
This forgets to square the inner radius.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
